items.front: For EDST, what does the current plan represent?
items.back: What EAS predicts the aircraft will fly, including clearances not yet issued. EDST uses it to model the trajectory and detect conflicts.

items.front: Why must automation entries be made in EDST without delay?
items.back: Conflict-probe accuracy depends on current trajectory data such as route, interim altitude, restrictions, and aircraft type.

items.front: How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?
items.back: Record it via the ERAM Hold Data Menu, Hold View, a hold message, or manually on strips or approved worksheets under the facility standard.

items.front: What is the ESDT overdue-aircraft notification based on?
items.back: Radar track data. Updating the route of flight removes the notification, but the overdue-aircraft procedures still apply when the alert is received.

items.front: What is the Graphic Plan Display allowed to do operationally?
items.back: Aid situational awareness and strategic planning only. It is not a substitute for track position, Mode C, radar ID, transfer, separation, correlation, or point-outs.

items.front: Can trajectory-based positions be used for separation?
items.back: NO. Do not use trajectory-based positions as a substitute for radar track position. Do not use trajectory-based altitude in lieu of Mode C. Per 13-1-13(b-c).

items.front: If forecast wind data are unavailable, how should EDST conflict probe and trial planning be handled?
items.back: Continue using them, but recognize that alert and trajectory data may be affected.

items.front: If connectivity to an adjacent ERAM facility is lost, how should EDST still be used?
items.back: Continue using EDST, but recognize that alert data may be affected.

items.front: During a surveillance or flight-data outage, how may electronic flight data still be used?
items.back: Use it to support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures.

items.front: What are the ERAM Airspace Configuration Elements, and what may the sector have to do with them?
items.back: They are Special Activity Airspace, Airport Stream Filters, and adapted restrictions. If assigned locally, keep their status current and notify the supervisor when it changes.

items.front: What must happen before transferring control of an aircraft that has Stop Probe activated?
items.back: Restart conflict probe for that aircraft before transfer of control unless otherwise coordinated. This does not apply to an aircraft entering a non-EDST facility.

items.front: What must be done with EDST conflict probe alerts?
items.back: Actively scan for predicted aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace alerts. Evaluate and take appropriate action as early as practical per duty priorities. Per 13-1-2(a-c).

items.front: When EDST is available and priorities permit, what is trial planning used to evaluate?
items.back: Predicted-conflict solutions, user requests, and whether removing a flight-direction constraint or static restriction is feasible.

items.front: If a user-request trial plan shows no alerts, what should normally happen next?
items.back: Make every effort to grant the request unless the change is likely to adversely affect operations in another sector.

items.front: What is the EDST sector team's primary source of flight data?
items.back: The Aircraft List (ACL).

items.front: If a flight plan changes after the Coordination Menu was used, when is the yellow coordination coding removed?
items.back: After the flight-plan change is addressed and any appropriate action has been taken.

items.front: What may be used to annotate holding instructions for a flight in hold?
items.back: Use Hold View, the Hold Data Menu, a hold message, a flight progress strip, or a facility-approved worksheet, as directed locally.

items.front: What limitation applies when control information is entered in the ACL or DL Free Text Area?
items.back: It is for reference only and does not pass on handoff. Keep it visible and update or delete it when it is no longer relevant.

items.front: When may IAFDOF, UTM, or EDCT coding be acknowledged and removed?
items.back: Only after the required action or coordination has actually been completed.

items.front: What does the ATOP ATC system provide in designated en route or oceanic airspace?
items.back: Surveillance and flight-data processing with decision support tools used to establish, monitor, and maintain separation between aircraft and between aircraft and airspace or terrain.

items.front: When ATOP conflict probe is in use, what should controllers assume about the separation calculations?
items.back: Assume the probe calculations are accurate and use the results unless an authorized override condition applies or the separation standard is one probe does not support.

items.front: Which reduced-separation flags must controllers manage on ATOP electronic flight strips?
items.back: Use the appropriate flags for MNT, reduced MNT, distance-based longitudinal, and RVSM, and keep annotations and restrictions current.

items.front: If there is doubt that a CPDLC clearance reached the aircraft, what must the controller do with that airspace?
items.back: Continue protecting the airspace tied to the clearance until the crew gives the expected operational response, and take action to confirm receipt if needed.

items.front: What does a pink weather-deviation advisory with a zero or positive plus-or-minus value indicate?
items.back: The aircraft pair is at or close to the required spacing, so the proposed weather deviation needs further evaluation before approval.

items.front: When is automated coordination between ATOP sectors not complete coordination by itself?
items.back: When the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector, or when facility directives or an LOA require something more.

items.front: What governs multiple-controller operation at an ATOP workstation?
items.back: All ATC tasks still have to be completed by priority of duties, and the multiple-person operation must follow facility directives.
